coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as high-effective inhibitors for carbon steel in acidic medium
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Shuyun
Liu, Dan
Wang, Tianxiang
Ma, Aijing
Liu, Chengwei
Zhuang, Xupin
Ding, Hui
Mamba, Bhekie B.
Gui, Jianzhou
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of nanomaterials, are found to be good inhibitors. Given that there is no clear mechanistic explanation in the literature on how the CDs inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel in H2SO4 medium. Eco-friendly nitrogen-doped functionalized carbon dots (N-CDs) were facilely synthesized via the one-step hydrothermal reaction of two low-cost raw materials with multiple functional groups, i.e., citric acid and urea. Their corrosion inhibition behavior was systematically investigated via electrochemical methods and surface analysis. The resultant N-CDs were found to be an effective corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 30 mg/L, with the inhibition efficiency exceeding 95 % at 298 K. The N-CDs can be adsorbed onto the steel surface by forming a barrier to prevent/ reduce the attack of corrosive ions, which follows the Langmuir adsorption model. Their inhibition mechanism was also proposed, providing a guide to search for new nanomaterials for metal against corrosion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Acid corrosion
Inhibitor
Carbon steel
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FTIR, UV–vis, XRD, and TEM analysis of N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation schematic of the N-CDs with citric acid and urea as precursors.
FTIR spectrum (a), UV–vis spetrum (b), PL emission spetrum (c), XRD pattern (d), and TEM image (e) of the N-CDs.
XPS survey of the N-CDs: XPS-full spectrum (A); C 1s (B); N 1s (C); O 1s spectrum (D).
Polarization plots for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution uninhibited and inhibited with different concentrations of the N-CDs.
Nyquist plots for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution uninhibited and inhibited with different concentrations of the N-CDs (a) and the equivalent circuit model employed to fit the EIS data (b).
Bode (a) and phase angle (b) plots for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution uninhibited and inhibited with different concentrations of the N-CDs.
Corrosion rate (a) of carbon steel and inhibition efficiency (b) of the N-CDs with different concentrations in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.
SEM images of the steel surfaces before treatment (a), after 2 h of immersion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution without N-CDs(b), and with 30 mg/L N-CDs(c).
2D (a) and 3D (b) AFM images of the steel immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution for 2 h at 298 K, and height profile (c) and (d) made along the marked line in (a).
2D (a) and 3D (b) AFM images of the steel immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution without the N-CDs for 2 h at 298 K, height profile (c) made along the marked line in (a).

The XPS spectra of the steel surface in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution after 2 h of immersion uninhibited and inhibited with 30 mg/L N-CDs: Full-XPS spectrum (a); N 1s spectrum of steel surface in solution inhibited with N-CDs (b).
Langmuir adsorption plots for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution uninhibited and inhibited by different concentrations of the N-CDs: using PDP data (a), using EIS data (b).
The proposed scheme for the adsorption and anti-corrosion mechanism of the N-CDs on the Fe surface in acid medium.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from PDP curves for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution uninhibited and inhibited with different concentrations of the N-CDs at 298 K.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from Nyquist plots for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution uninhibited and inhibited with different concentrations of the N-CDs at 298 K.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as high-effective inhibitors for carbon steel in acidic medium

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Technology and Process Engineering
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Eco-friendly carbon dots (CDs), with N-doped functionalized groups, were found to be an effective corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium. Their corrosion inhibition behavior was systematically investigated by electrochemical method and micromorphology analysis. The results showed that the N-CDs could suppress the anodic and cathodic reaction by adsorption to form a barrier onto the steel surface, preventing the transportation of corrosive particles in the bulk solution, which follows the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. It can further increase the inhibition efficiency by increasing the N-CDs amount. The inhibition mechanism was also proposed, which could provide a guide to search for new nanomaterials for metal against corrosion.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of nanomaterials, are found to be good inhibitors. Given that there is no clear mechanistic explanation in the literature on how the CDs inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel in H2SO4 medium. Eco-friendly nitrogen-doped functionalized carbon dots (N-CDs) were facilely synthesized via the one-step hydrothermal reaction of two low-cost raw materials with multiple functional groups, i.e., citric acid and urea. Their corrosion inhibition behavior was systematically investigated via electrochemical methods and surface analysis. The resultant N-CDs were found to be an effective corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 30 mg/L, with the inhibition efficiency exceeding 95 % at 298 K. The N-CDs can be adsorbed onto the steel surface by forming a barrier to prevent/ reduce the attack of corrosive ions, which follows the Langmuir adsorption model. Their inhibition mechanism was also proposed, providing a guide to search for new nanomaterials for metal against corrosion.

ce:section.ce:para: The degradation of metal caused by corrosion can result in enormous economic loss and severe life safety [1]. Common acid aggressive media, such as sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, phosphoric acid, and hydrofluoric acid, are extensively applied in the industries during the processes of oil well acidification, chemical cleaning, and processing, and pickling, etc. [2]. The protection strategy of the metal substrate during the mentioned above processes is therefore important. Among numerous anti-corrosion methods, the addition of corrosion inhibitors is considered to be an effective, simple, and low-cost strategy for preventing or alleviating the corrosion of the steel substrate [3,4]. The inhibition efficiency of an inhibitor depends on the structural characteristics of the inhibitor, the nature of the metal surface, and the aggressive environment, etc. Some traditional high-efficiency corrosion inhibitors, such as chromate, and phosphorus-containing compounds, are difficult to biodegrade and exhibit a certain degree of toxicity [5]. In addition, organic chemicals, which usually contains some active adsorption centers including heteroatoms (e.g., N, S, O, and P), aromatic rings, heterocyclic rings, and unsaturated bonds, are found to be effective inhibitors for the metal in acidic media, which can form adsorption film onto the metal surface by physisorption or chemisorption, or the combination of them, via creating a barrier against diffusion of aggressive species in bulk solution toward the metal surface [6,7]. However, many of these inhibitors are toxic, even though they exhibit excellent inhibition performance. Therefore, it is necessary to develop environment-friendly inhibitors to replace these corrosion inhibitors [8].

An essential requirement to a promising inhibitor for use is a low-cost, stable, sustainable, and non-toxic product. Carbon nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs), have received significant attention. They are promising zero dimension nanomaterials possessing many desirable properties for this purpose, such as inexpensive, good water solubility, simple synthetic routes, low-toxicity, and tunable surface chemistry, biocompatibility, etc. [9–11]. Heteroatom doping is a simple and effective strategy to improve the properties of CDs. Researchers recently reported that nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) could effectively inhibit steel corrosion in HCl solution [12–14]. Cui et al. reported an N-CDs as the inhibitor using antibiotic aminosalicylic acid as a precursor for the first time against carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution, which shows effective inhibition performance (the maximum inhibition efficiency is about 90 %) [12]. Ye et al. reported two carbon dots, one was derived from methacrylic acids and n-butylamine, and the other one was prepared with citric acid and l-histidine as precursors. Both CDs show high inhibition efficiencies for carbon steel in HCl solution (>90 %) at the concentration of 200 mg/L [13,14]. However, the inhibition mechanism of the CDs is still unclear, and there are few studies on CDs as inhibitors for carbon steel used in H2SO4 medium. Adsorption of an inhibitor could be influenced by the nature of anions in acidic media [15]. The Cl− ions in hydrochloric acid can cause the induced characteristic adsorption, which may help inhibitors absorb onto the steel surfaces, showing synergistic effect, thus enhance the inhibition [12]. While SO4 2- ions in sulfuric acid have a weaker tendency to be adsorbed than Cl− ions [15]. In addition, the lesser interference of SO4 2- ions with the adsorbed protonated positively charged form of an inhibitor may lead to less adsorption. Herein, how is the inhibition performance of N-CDs and their inhibition mechanism? Moreover, how the N-CDs nanostructure, rich in multiple functional groups, affects the corrosion inhibition?

According to the above references and our previous work on corrosion inhibitors, this study aims to investigate that the nitrogen-doped and water-soluble functionalized N-CDs synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method from common low-cost and eco-friendly raw materials with multiple functional groups, and its effective inhibition for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, giving insights into the possible inhibition mechanism. Due to the existence of multiple functional groups, such as −OH, –NH2, –CO, and −COOH of the two raw materials, it is hypothesized herein that this N-CDs inhibitor has both functional groups and multiple bonds, and can improve adsorption ability and thus obtain high anti-corrosion performance towards the steel. The synthesized N-CDs have been characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, UV–vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) etc. Then inhibition performance of synthesized N-CDs was systematically investigated by potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Weight loss measurement, and some surface characterization methods, such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Atomic force microscope (AFM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), providing deep insights into the inhibition mechanism.

Citric acid, 98 wt% sulfuric acid, and urea were purchased from Adamas-beta and used as received without other purification. Carbon steel was used in this work. The steel samples were processed into the dimensions of 10 × 10 × 2 mm (for electrochemical tests and immersion tests) and 50 × 10 × 2 mm with 0.5 mm bore diameter (for weight loss tests) with weight percentage composition of C, 0.4–0.5; Ni, ≤0.3; Cr, ≤0.2; Si, 0.2–0.4; Cu, ≤0.2; Mn, 0.5–0.8; Fe for balance.

Compared with some previous CDs inhibitors, the cost of raw materials used in this work is the lowest, [12–14] and a similar hydrothermal preparation method of N-CDs was applied, with citric acid and urea as a carbon source and nitrogen precursor, respectively, which has been used as a molecular imprinted sensor and a mitochondria-targeted fuorescent probe, respectively [16,17]. The modified synthetic route for a better corrosion inhibitor was shown in Fig. 1 . Typically, 6.0 g citric acid and 2.0 g urea were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon autoclave and keep it at 200 °C for 6 h. After the temperature was cooling down, the as-prepared solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and further refined through a filter membrane (with a pore diameter of 0.2 um). Then, the solution was dried at 60 °C under vacuum to obtain the black N-CDs. The N-CDs were stored in a dry and dark place at low temperatures for next use.

Morphology of N-CDs was observed by transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, JEM-2100 F). First, the N-CDs were dissolved in ethyl alcohol, then titrated on the copper mesh and dried for TEM observation. The chemical structure was evaluated using KBr disks from 4000 to 500 cm−1 by FT-IR spectra (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS50 FT-IR). The UV–vis spectra were obtained using quartz cells of light pass length 1 cm in the 190–800 nm range using UV–vis spectrophotometry (Thermo Scientific™ Evolution 300). Fluorescence (PL) spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-380, TianjinGangdong Sci.&Tech. Development. Co., Ltd). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed with Rigaku miniplex X-ray diffractometer. XPS was collected on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer using an Al Kα anode (1486.6 eV), and the binding energy reference was set at the C 1s peak of carbon, 284.6 eV.

The corrosive medium was prepared by 0.5 M H2SO4 solution without N-CDs/ with different concentrations (10, 20, 25, and 30 mg/L) of N-CDs. The 0.5 M H2SO4 solution was prepared by diluting 98 wt% H2SO4 with deionized water.

Electrochemical measurements for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution in the absence and presence of N-CDs were performed using an electrochemical workstation (Reference 3000, Gamry Industry. Inc.) with a classic three electrodes system.

The working electrode (WE) is the above-mentioned carbon steel with an exposed surface area of 1 cm2, while the rest was embedded by epoxy resin. An Ag/AgCl electrode solution connected via a Luggin’s capillary filled with saturated KCl solution is the reference electrode, and a Pt plate is used as the counter electrode (C.E.). The WE was abraded with wet silicon carbide abrasive paper of steadily increasing grade of #360, #600, #1000, #1500, and #2000, then rinsed with distilled water, and then degreased with anhydrous isopropanol, and finally dried for immediate use. The working electrode was firstly immersed into the test solution for 60 min to achieve the steady state of open circuit potential (E ocp). Then electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out, and then successively followed by the potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) tests. PDP tests were performed at ±250 mV versus Ag/AgCl electrode at corrosion potential (E corr) at a constant sweep rate of 0.5 mV s−1. The E corr and corrosion current density were collected by extrapolation at Tafel segments of the obtained PDP curves. EIS tests were recorded at the E ocp in a frequency range of 100 kHz −50 mHz, with an amplitude perturbation of 10 mV. ZSimpWin software was used for EIS data fitting. All experiments were tested in static states without removing oxygen.

The inhibition efficiency obtained from PDP results was calculated as follows: [18]
(1)
IE PDP% = (i corr – i corr
(
N-CDs))/i corr × 100
where IE PDP% refers to the inhibition efficiency gained from PDP tests, and i corr and i corr(N-CDs) are the corrosion current densities uninhibited or inhibited by N-CDs inhibitor, respectively.

The inhibition efficiency obtained by the EIS results was defined using the following expression: [18]
(2)
IE EIS% = (R ct(N-CDs) −R ct)/R ct(N-CDs) × 100
where IE EIS% refers to the inhibition efficiency obtained from EIS tests, and R ct and R ct(N-CDs) are the charge transfer resistances in the absence and presence of N-CDs inhibitor, respectively.

Carbon steel specimens were sequentially abraded with wet silicon carbide abrasive paper (#360, #600, #1000, #1500, and #2000) to achieve a reproducible surface, then cleaned with distilled water and anhydrous isopropanol, respectively, dried and kept in a desiccator for use. After being weighed accurately by an analytical balance (accuracy±0.1 mg), gravimetric experiments were performed for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without various concentrations (0, 10, 20, 25, and 30 mg/L) of N-CDs at 298 K for 2 h. After the gravimetric tests, the steel surface was thoroughly washed successively using distilled water and anhydrous ethanol. The corrosion products on the steel surface were removed according to the ASTM standard G1-03 [19]. Then the steel surface was dried and reweighed.
In order to ensure reproducibility, the measurements were carried out in triplicate for each concentration.

The corrosion rate (V c) in mm/a was calculated as follows [20]:
(3)
V c=(W 0-W 1)/tA
where W 0 and W 1 are the weight of steel specimens before and after immersion in corrosive solution, respectively, t is the immersion time (h), and A is the exposed surface area (cm2) of tested specimens.

The inhibition efficiency (IE w) is calculated as follows [20]:
(4)
IEw%=(V 0 -V)/V 0 × 100
where V 0 and V are the values of corrosion rate in the absence and presence of inhibitor, respectively.

Morphology studies of the steel surfaces after immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution in the absence and presence of N-CDs were performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (BRUKER Nano, X Flash Detector 5010), and the component of protection film formed on the steel surface was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB 250 model). Atomic force microscope (AFM) (Hitachi AFM5100 N) was used to assess the roughness characteristics of the specimens. Prior to each immersion experiment before morphology studies, the surfaces of the steel specimens were abraded, cleaned and dried as previously described, and then immediately immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without N-CDs inhibitor for 2 h. After immersion, the steel specimens were rinsed with acetone, dried with cool air, and stored in a small-scale desiccator before use, and then submitted for surface examination. i.e. SEM, AFM, and XPS measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR, UV–vis, XRD, and TEM analysis of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (a) shows the chemical structure of N-CDs characterized by FTIR spectroscopy. The broad peak at 3429 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of –O-H and the vibration of –N-H, while the peaks at 2958 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of -C-H bonds. Peaks at 1643 and 1570 cm−1 are associated with the CO and CN stretching. The vibrational peak at 1167 cm−1 is corresponded to the CH (wagging) [21]. The formation of CN and CN bonds suggested that the N-CDs was successfully synthesized.20 As displayed in the UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 2(b)), the N-CDs show a broad absorption peak at around 340 nm. Fluorescence spectrum was shown in Fig. 2(c). The emission wavelength is around 425 nm when it is excited at the wavelength of 330 nm, which is consistent with the previous report [17]. The insets of Fig. 2(c) shows the optical photographs of the N-CDs aqueous solution, which indicates that it is colorless under visible light and can emit blue luminescence when irradiated with a UV lamp (365 nm). The XRD in Fig. 2(d) shows that the N-CDs demonstrates a broad peak at around 23.4°, indicating that the N-CDs are amorphous materials ascribed to highly disordered carbon atoms. Fig. 2(e) shows the TEM of the as-prepared N-CDs. It can be seen that they are almost spherical nanoparticles with an average size of around 10−14 nm.

Fig. 3 shows the XPS spectra of the as-prepared NCD. As can be seen from the XPS full spectrum in Fig. 3(a), there are C, N, and O elements included in the N-CDs. In the C 1s spectra in Fig. 3(b), three peaks can be observed at 284.7 ± 0.1 (CC aliphatic bonds), 285.4 ± 0.1 (CN), and 288.1 ± 0.1 eV (CO) [22,23].

The N 1s spectra (Fig. 3(c)) show three main peaks. The peak at 398.2 eV can be assigned to pyridinelike N, the peak at 399.5 eV is assign to pyrrole-like N, and the peak at 401.1 eV can be ascribed to graphitic N [24].

The O 1s spectra (Fig. 3(d)) show two peaks. One peak at 198.3 ± 0.1 eV can be ascribed to C O, and the other peak at 199.9 ± 0.1 eV, is attributed to CH/ COC [25]. The XPS results were in agreement with the FTIR analysis.

Fig. 4 shows the PDP curves, and the corresponding electrochemical parameters, including corrosion current density (i corr), corrosion potentials (E corr), inhibition efficiency (IE%), anodic slope (β a), and cathodic slope (β c), are listed in Table 1 . As can be seen, in the presence of N-CDs, all the i corr values including the cathodic and anodic current densities decrease, which indicates that both the cathodic dissolution of Fe and the anodic hydrogen evolution reactions are inhibited. The desorption potentials on the anodic polarization curves occur, suggesting the actual adsorption of the N-CDs. In addition, In the presence of N-CDs, the corrosion current densities decrease with inhibitor concentration, and the maximum E corr in the presence of the N-CDs inhibitor shift less than ±85 mV, which indicates that the inhibitor acts as a mixed-type inhibitor that not only retard anodic but also cathodic reaction [26].

In Table 1, the i corr values decrease after the addition of N-CDs and show a downtrend with increasing the inhibitor concentration, which indicates that the corrosion rates decrease and the IE% enhances with N-CDs concentration.
As expected, IE PDP%, in the presence of N-CDs, increases with N-CDs concentration and reaches the maximum values of 97.8 % at the tested maximum concentration of 30 mg/L.

The Nyquist and relevant Bode plots for carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution are displayed in Figs. 5 (a) and 6 , respectively. It is clear in Fig. 5 that each plot shows a single capacitive depressed semicircle, which is associated with charge transfer and electrical double layer. This suggests that the corrosion processes are mainly controlled by the charge transfer process, [27] while the surface roughness or surface inhomogeneity of the steel surface makes the capacitive semicircle depressed with its circular-center located under the x-axis [28].

The diameter of the semicircles increases with the addition of N-CDs and continuously increases in concentration. This indicates that the addition of N-CDs can protect the steel and inhibit the charge transfer process.

As seen from Bode plots in Fig. 6(a), in the entire frequency range, the impedance modulus increases with the addition of N-CDs, and continuously increases with N-CDs concentration. Besides, all the phase angles show one phase peak with the maximum phase angles increase as the N-CDs concentration increases (Fig. 6(b)). These results indicate that there is merely one time constant related to the charge transfer and electrical double-layer formation at the surface/solution interface, and the addition of N-CDs can show the effective protective ability for the steel.

Based on the above results, the one-time-constant equivalent circuit in Fig. 5b was used to fit the EIS data. The circuit is composed of solution resistance (R s), charge transfer resistance (R ct), and constant-phase angle element of a double layer (CPE/Q) that is used to replace ideal double-layer capacitance (C dl) due to the non-ideal capacitance behavior of the inhomogeneous electrode [29].

The impedance function of CPE can be calculated as follows: [30]
(5)
Z CPE= Y 0 −1 (jω) -n
where Y 0 is the CPE constant, n (-1 ≤ n ≤ 1) refers to the CPE exponent, j (j= (-1)1/2) refers to an imaginary number, and ω (ω=2πf) represents the angular frequency in rad−1.

C dl can be calculated by the following equation: [31]
(6)
C dl= Y 0 (2πf max) n−1
where f max refers to the maximum frequency of the impedance spectrum.

ω max can also be obtained by the following equation: [31,32]
(7)
C dl = ε 0 ε S/d
where d refers to the thickness of the double layer, ε 0 is a constant of the permittivity of air, ε is the local dielectric constant, and S represents the area of the WE surface.

Table 2 shows the electrochemical parameters derived from EIS data. It can be seen that R ct values increase while C dl values generally decrease with the addition of N-CDs, compared to that in the uninhibited medium. Moreover, this effect enhances with increasing N-CDs concentration. These results indicate a decrease in ε and an increase in d, which is attributed to the adsorption of the N-CDs on the steel surface. Accordingly, a conclusion can be drawn that the N-CDs molecules interact with the steel by adsorption simultaneously with the displacement of H2O molecules and/or other ions initially adsorbed onto the steel surface, leading to a reduction of the corrosion rate.

As a result, The inhibition efficiency increases with increasing the N-CDs concentration, reaching up to 95.7 % for 30 mg/L from 62.1 % for 10 mg/L at 298 K. The inhibition performance shows a good agreement with those obtained from the above PDP results.

In order to find out the effect of N-CDs against acid corrosion, the weight loss measurements were carried out, and the results were shown in Fig. 7 . It can be observed that the addition of N-CDs decreases the corrosion of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Meanwhile, in the presence of the N-CDs, the corrosion rate of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution decreases with the increase of N-CDs concentration, and the lowest corrosion rate (0.26 mg cm−2 h-1) can be obtained, while the inhibition efficiency increases with N-CDs concentration and reached 93.0 % at 30 mg/L. This was attributed to the increased adsorption quantity and/or increased surface coverage area of N-CDs at the steel surface/solution interface, leading to a less corrosive attack towards carbon steel. Moreover, when adding a higher concentration of N-CDs, the inhibition efficiency is growing more slowly, suggesting the tendency towards adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The results obtained from the weight loss measurements are consistent with those obtained from the above PDP and EIS experiments.

Fig. 8 shows SEM images for the carbon steel before and after immersion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution in the absence and presence of 30 mg/L N-CDs for two hours. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the freshly grinded steel surface is relatively smooth with some polishing marks. In Fig. 8(b), the steel surface was seriously corroded with many deep cracks in 0.5 M H2SO4 in the absence of N-CDs. However, corrosion damage of the steel surface after immersion in inhibited solution distinctly decreased (Fig. 8(c)) with fewer corrosion artifacts compared to that corroded one in uninhibited solution. This can be attributed to the formation of a protective layer in the presence of N-CDs.

The AFM images of the steel specimens immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution in the absence and presence of 30 mg/L N-CDs for 2 h at 298 K are illustrated in Figs. 9 and 10
, respectively.

The steel surface (Fig. 9a and b) immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution shows the severe porous structure with large and deep pits, compared with that protected surface (Fig. 10a and b) by the N-CDs. This is further confirmed by the height profile of the steel surface, which is made along the marked line. The profile of the corroded surface (Fig. 9c) shows a large fluctuation of about 2000 nm, while a slight fluctuation within 600 nm can be observed for the protected surface (Fig. 10c and d). Moreover, the average roughness (Ra) decreases from 334.5 nm (the steel surface in uninhibited solution) to 55.3 nm (the surface in inhibited solution). This indicates that the N-CDs can remarkably decrease the corrosion rate.

In order to understand the chemical nature and the adsorption type of the protective film formed on the steel surface, XPS technology of the steel surface after immersion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution in the absence and presence of 30 mg/L N-CDs for 2 h was performed, and the results are shown in Fig. 11 . Fig. 11(a) shows the full XPS spectra of the steel uninhibited and inhibited with 30 mg/L N-CDs. Besides C, O, Fe, and S atoms, N atoms from the N-CDs are detected in the presence of N-CDs. This indicates that the N-CDs molecules are successfully adsorbed onto the steel surface. As shown in Fig. 11(b), the N 1s spectrum is fitted into three peaks, which are located at 398.7, 399.9, and 401.2 eV. The peak at 398.7 eV is assigned to the unprotonated N atom (C-N) [33]. The peak at 399.9 eV corresponds to the coordinate N with the steel surface (Fe-N) [34]. The peak at 401.2 eV is related to the protonation of N (N+H) [35]. This finding implies that the N-CDs inhibitor is adsorbed onto the steel surface via both chemical (seen from C-N and Fe-N) and physical (N+H) adsorption.
Inhibition mechanisms for most organic inhibitors in acidic media are considered to be the adsorption of inhibitor molecules onto metal surfaces, accompanied by the displacement of water molecules.

Adsorption mechanisms include physical adsorption and chemisorption or both. Thus, it is of great importance to find out the appropriate adsorption isotherms for understanding the interaction mechanism between inhibitor molecules and the steel surface.

Many common adsorption isotherms such as the Langmuir, Frumkin, Temkin, and Frundlich models have been tested. Among them, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model was found to be the best to fit so as to explain the adsorption of the N-CDs. This adsorption isotherm is as follows: [36]
(8)
C inh/θ = 1/K ads+C inh
where C inh is inhibitor concentration, θ, is defined as θ=IE%/100, represents the surface coverage caused by the inhibitor molecule, and K ads is the equilibrium constant.

Fig. 12 shows the plot of C inh/θ versus C inh. The slope of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm for the N-CDs is near one, and the linear correlation coefficient (R 2) reaching above 0.98. The standard free energy (ΔG ɵ ads) could be obtained by the following equation: [37]
(9) ΔG ɵ ads= -RT ln (CH2O K ads)
where CH2O is 1000 g/L, R refers to the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1) and T represents the thermodynamic temperature in Kelvin.

K ads value can be used to judge the adsorption ability of the N-CDs inhibitor. The higher the K ads value, the stronger the adsorption ability of the N-CDs inhibitor. The K ads values are 178.6 and 123.5 g/L obtained from PDP and EIS results, respectively. ΔG ɵ ads value could be used to judge the adsorption type. When ΔG°ads > −20 kJ mol−1, physical adsorption occurs. When ΔG°ads < −40 kJ mol−1, it indicates chemisorption are dominated, and if ΔG ɵ ads is between -0 kJ mol−1 and -20 kJ mol−1, it implies combined physical and chemical adsorption [38]. If ΔG ɵ ads is negative, it means that the adsorption process is spontaneous, vice versa. In this case, ΔG ɵ ads values for the N-CDs are -30.0 and -29.0 kJ mol-1 obtained from PDP and EIS results, respectively, suggesting that the adsorption process is spontaneous, and both physical and chemical adsorptions exist at the steel/solution interface.

Given that the tested solutions were not degassed during experiments, the amount of solubilized oxygen and carbon dioxide present can be neglected compared to H+, which is rich in the solution. Therefore, the major cause of corrosion is H+. The anodic reaction of carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution is Fe dissolution, and the cathodic reaction is the hydrogen evolution reaction. When the as-prepared N-CDs inhibitors are added, the inhibition mechanism for the N-CDs in acidic media is considered to be forming a protection film by the adsorption of the inhibitor molecules onto the metal surface to retard corrosion, as displayed in Fig. 13 . From the above analysis, the combined physical adsorption and chemisorption of these N-CDs contributed to the effective inhibition. The N-CDs molecules can first adsorb onto the steel surface via electrostatic interaction (physical adsorption). Then chemisorption occurs by sharing electrons between N-CDs and the steel surface. The chemical structure and charge distribution of the N-CDs play an important role in the adsorption process. The N-CDs are composed of pyridine-N, pyrrole-like N, graphitic N atom, and O atoms. The pyridine-N and pyrrole-like N with lone electron pairs are responsible for the chemical adsorption, which can interact with the unfilled 3d orbital of the Fe atom to form a coordination bonding. Meanwhile, the electron from the unfilled 3d of Fe atom may be transferred to the vacant π* (anti-bonding) orbital of N-CDs, strengthening adsorption on the steel surface. However, for the remaining graphitic N atom of the N-CDs, even though it can not constitute a coordination bond with the steel surface, but can form a shield via electrostatic interaction. The O atom with a lone electron pair can also facilitate the adsorption of N-CDs onto the steel surface against acid attack, resulting in effective inhibition performance. Besides, the hydrophobic structure (carbon-rich) of the N-CDs can also help increase the inhibition performance via building a shield to reduce the attack of aggressive ions in bulk solution.

Besides the chemical and electronic structures of the N-CDs themselves, the inhibition efficiency also depends on the nature of the steel surface, and composition of the aggressive environment, etc. As stated in other papers, the surface of the carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution possesses a net positive charge, [39] which will firstly adsorb the anion such as SO4 2− in solution, and thus create an excess negative charge and favor cation adsorption [40]. In the presence of N-CDs, the protonation of N in CDs can be adsorbed onto the steel surface after the anion (SO4 2−) adsorption, due to the electrostatic interactions.
Therefore, The effective physical adsorption and chemisorption caused by the N-CDs for carbon steel in H2SO4 solution contributed to the high surface coverage and hence high inhibition.

Environmental-friendly N-doped functionalized carbon dots (N-CDs) were facilely synthesized with simple hydrothermal method from low-cost raw materials with multiple functional groups, i.e., citric acid and urea, and then characterized by various methods. Their inhibition performance to carbon steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution was systematically studied, and the inhibition mechanism has been proposed. The conclusions were summarized as follows:
(1) N-CDs can effectively inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel in the H2SO4 solution with the inhibition efficiency as high as 97.8 % at 298 K at 30 mg/L.

(2) Surface morphology observation agrees well with electrochemical results and further demonstrates that the N-CDs form an adsorption protective film on the steel surface, thus forming a barrier against the corrosive attack of aggressive ions to the steel surface in solution.

(3) The high inhibition performance of the N-CDs was attributed to their special structure: carbon-rich structure and multiple functional groups confirmed by XPS, such as pyridine-N, pyrrole-like N, graphitic N atom, and O atoms, thus causing an excellent protective layer formed.

(4) The adsorption of N-CDs combines physical adsorption and chemisorption simultaneously. The N and O atoms with lone electron pairs formed coordination bonds with the Fe atoms on the steel surface, contributing to the chemisorption; however, the protonation of N in the N-CDs leads to physical adsorption.

Shuyun Cao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Dan Liu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Tianxiang Wang: Validation, Investigation. Aijing Ma: Formal analysis. Chengwei Liu: Formal analysis. Xupin Zhuang: Formal analysis. Hui Ding: Formal analysis. Bhekie B. Mamba: Formal analysis. Jianzhou Gui: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
